title: Capturing many-body effects in electrical conductivity of warm dense matter
authors: Brian P. Robinson, Alina Kononov, Lucas J. Stanek, Andrew D. Baczewski, André Schleife, Stephanie B. Hansen
abstract: Conductivity models for warm dense matter inform simulations of planetary structure and fusion experiments. State-of-the-art conductivity calculations based on density functional theory approximate many-body physics and neglect electron-electron scattering lifetimes. We introduce a many-body framework for electrical conductivity using the GW approximation of the electronic self-energy. For beryllium, improved transition energies yield a surprisingly large reduction in low-temperature DC conductivity, while electron-electron scattering primarily reduces high-temperature DC conductivity.
summary: This paper introduces a many-body GW framework to correct the inaccuracies of standard DFT in predicting the conductivity of warm dense matter. It demonstrates that accounting for electron-electron scattering and quasiparticle energy shifts is crucial for accurately modeling transport in extreme density and temperature regimes.
main_problem: Standard Density Functional Theory (DFT) fails to accurately predict the electrical conductivity of warm dense matter because it neglects many-body effects, specifically electron-electron scattering lifetimes.
main_result: The GW approximation reveals that energy corrections widen the pseudogap (reducing low-temperature conductivity) and electron-electron scattering increases broadening (reducing high-temperature conductivity), leading to a 2-5x reduction in predicted DC conductivity compared to KS-DFT.
method: The authors use the GW approximation for electronic self-energy within the Kubo-Greenwood formalism, incorporating both quasiparticle energy shifts and transition-dependent lifetime broadening.
model_or_system: The study focuses on liquid beryllium (Be) in the warm dense matter regime at near-solid density (1.97 g/cm3), covering both thermalized and isochorically heated states.
key_observables: DC electrical conductivity, optical conductivity, electronic density of states (DOS), and quasiparticle lifetimes.
important_parameters: Electronic temperature (0.2 eV to 6.8 eV), mass density (1.97 g/cm3), and the pseudogap width.
main_assumptions: The study uses the G0W0 single-shot approximation and assumes the validity of the Kubo-Greenwood formalism for transport properties.
figures_summary: Figures compare optical and density of states between KS-DFT and GW models, illustrate the temperature dependence of GW broadening, and show the systematic reduction of DC conductivity across various temperature regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the failure of DFT in WDM, presents the GW many-body framework, details the application to beryllium, analyzes low- vs. high-temperature effects, and compares results to existing models like ETHOS.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Electronic densities of states for 1.97 g/cm3 Be at temperatures of (a) 0.2 and (b) 6.8 eV predicted by KS-DFT (red), GW (blue), and the free-electron gas model (yellow). The shaded regions show each DOS weighted by Fermi-Dirac occupations. In (c), the GW broadening along EGW i = µ at both temperatures is compared to a typical static value of 0.1 eV (dashed line). The GW broadening for all energy transitions around the chemical potential are illustrated in Fig. S3 of the SM.

FIG. 3. The temperature dependence of DC conductivity for Be at 1.97 g/cm3 computed using conventional KS-DFT (red circles), GW-corrected eigenvalues (blue squares), and GW-corrected dynamic broadening (yellow triangles). Solid curves correspond to the ETHOS [12] parameterization of the Lee-Moore-Desjarlais model [33] constrained by our KS-DFT (red), GW-corrected eigenvalues (blue), and GW-corrected dynamic broadening (yellow) data. The dashed black line is the interpolated and extrapolated ETHOS data by Stanek et al. [12], and the dashed light blue curve shows predictions from DFT-AA. Error bars estimate both the statistical and systematic errors in the predictions as described in Sec. S1...
